learning_objectives: Define interactive storytelling and its relationship to traditional narrative
Identify the core elements that distinguish interactive fiction from linear media
Survey the history of interactive narrative from text adventures to modern AAA titles
readings: Interactive Storytelling for Video Games Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Interactive storytelling is the craft of building narratives that respond to player choice. Unlike film or literature, game narratives must account for agency, consequence, and emergent meaning.
key_concepts: Ludology vs. narratology debate
Ergodic literature and cybertext theory
Player agency as a narrative force
Diegetic vs. non-diegetic storytelling
The spectrum from linear to fully emergent narrative
lecture_notes.demonstration: Play-through comparison of a branching scene in The Walking Dead vs. a cutscene-driven sequence in Final Fantasy, analyzing player agency in each

review.value: The degree to which a player's choices meaningfully alter the narrative outcome and their subjective experience of the story
Text requiring non-trivial effort from the reader to traverse, as defined by Espen Aarseth; games are a primary example

learning_objectives: Map the major branching narrative architectures used in games
Evaluate trade-offs between narrative depth and combinatorial complexity
Diagram a simple branching story using node-graph notation
readings: Salen & Zimmerman, Rules of Play Ch. 26

lecture_notes.introduction: Every interactive story has an underlying structure that determines how branches split, merge, and resolve. Choosing the right structure is a design decision that directly impacts scope, budget, and emotional impact.
key_concepts: Linear with optional side content (hub-and-spoke)
Binary branching trees and exponential growth problem
Foldback structures that reconverge after divergence
Parallel path narratives with late convergence
Quality-based narrative (Fallen London model)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagramming the branching structure of Inkle's 80 Days on a whiteboard, identifying fold-back points and tracking state variables

review.value: A branching narrative that allows divergence but forces reconvergence at key plot nodes, managing scope while preserving the feeling of choice
The exponential growth of unique story paths when every choice creates a permanent branch; the primary scaling challenge of branching narrative

learning_objectives: Compare major dialogue system architectures: menu-based, keyword, parser-based, and hybrid
Write dialogue that communicates character personality within branching constraints
Implement a basic dialogue tree in Ink scripting language
readings: Ink documentation: Writing with Ink tutorial

lecture_notes.introduction: Dialogue is the most common vehicle for interactive storytelling. A well-designed dialogue system balances expressiveness for the writer with clarity for the player and tractability for the developer.
key_concepts: Menu-based dialogue (Mass Effect wheel, Disco Elysium list)
Parser-based dialogue and natural language input
Bark systems for ambient and contextual NPC speech
Dialogue state machines and condition gating
Voice-over production pipeline and its constraints on branching
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live coding a multi-character dialogue scene in Ink with conditional branches based on player inventory and relationship values
setup_requirements: Sample dialogue template from course repository

review.value: A graph of dialogue nodes connected by edges whose traversal is gated by conditions such as player stats, inventory, or prior choices
Short, context-sensitive lines spoken by NPCs in response to game events without entering a full dialogue interface

learning_objectives: Apply environmental storytelling techniques to convey narrative without dialogue or text
Design a game world that communicates history, culture, and conflict through spatial arrangement
Analyze environmental storytelling in published games and identify specific techniques
readings: Environmental Storytelling in Games by Don Carson

lecture_notes.introduction: Environmental storytelling lets players discover narrative through exploration rather than exposition. A blood-stained room tells a story without a single line of dialogue. Mastering this technique reduces reliance on cutscenes and respects player agency.
key_concepts: Mise-en-scene applied to game environments
Found narrative objects: journals, audio logs, graffiti, environmental damage
Spatial storytelling and guided discovery
Layers of history in a single environment
Tension between authored placement and procedural generation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough analysis of environmental storytelling in Gone Home and Dark Souls, cataloguing every narrative object in a single room and mapping what each communicates
setup_requirements: Screenshot references from Gone Home, Dark Souls, and BioShock
Room layout template for in-class exercise

review.value: Story elements embedded in the environment as discoverable objects (letters, recordings, physical evidence) that the player pieces together
Using the arrangement and progression of spaces to guide the player through a narrative arc without explicit direction

learning_objectives: Design character arcs that function within branching narrative structures
Distinguish between authored protagonist arcs and player-projected identity
Write character development beats that adapt to player choices without losing coherence
readings: The Writer's Journey by Christopher Vogler, Ch. 1-3
Interactive Storytelling for Video Games Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Character arcs in interactive media face a fundamental tension: the writer wants a crafted transformation, but the player wants ownership of who the character becomes. The best interactive stories resolve this tension through careful structural design.
key_concepts: Three-act structure adapted for interactive pacing
Defined protagonist vs. blank-slate avatar
Companion characters as mirrors of player choice
Moral alignment systems and their narrative consequences
Arc-lock points: moments where character trajectory commits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of character arc design in The Witcher 3 (defined protagonist) vs. Fallout: New Vegas (blank-slate avatar), mapping how each handles player agency in character development

review.value: A character with a pre-established personality and backstory whose arc the player influences but does not wholly determine (e.g., Geralt of Rivia)
A narrative moment where a character's trajectory commits to a particular direction based on accumulated player choices, reducing future branching

class_schedule.topic: Quest Design and Narrative Tools Pipeline
learning_objectives: Structure quests that serve both gameplay and narrative goals simultaneously
Design quest chains with meaningful branching and consequences
Evaluate narrative middleware tools and select appropriate tooling for a project
readings: Yarn Spinner documentation: Getting Started guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Quests are the primary unit of interactive narrative delivery in most games. A well-designed quest weaves story, gameplay, and player choice into a single coherent experience. The tools you use to author quests directly shape what kinds of stories you can tell at scale.
key_concepts: Quest anatomy: hook, objectives, complications, resolution, reward
Side quests as world-building vehicles
Quest dependency graphs and non-linear quest ordering
Narrative middleware: Ink, Twine, Yarn Spinner, Articy:Draft
Localization pipeline and its impact on narrative scope
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete three-quest chain in Yarn Spinner with branching outcomes, conditional prerequisites, and a final consequence node that references all prior choices
setup_requirements: Yarn Spinner installed in Unity project

review.value: A directed graph showing prerequisite relationships between quests, enabling non-linear quest availability based on player progression and prior choices
Specialized authoring tools (Ink, Yarn Spinner, Articy:Draft) that allow writers to create branching content that integrates with game engines without writing engine code directly

questions.question: A designer is planning a narrative-driven RPG with 50 hours of gameplay. The team has budget for approximately 200,000 words of dialogue. Explain why a pure binary branching tree would be infeasible for this scope, and describe a specific alternative structure that would manage combinatorial growth while still giving players meaningful choices. Include a rough word-budget analysis to support your argument.
questions.answer: A pure binary branching tree doubles content at every decision point. With even 10 sequential binary choices, you would need 1,024 unique branches, far exceeding the 200,000-word budget if each branch contains meaningful content. A foldback structure solves this by allowing branches to diverge for a scene or chapter and then reconverge at key plot nodes, so the total word count grows linearly with the number of foldback segments rather than exponentially with the number of choices. For example, 10 foldback segments with 3 variants each require roughly 30 unique segments rather than 1,024 unique paths.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies exponential growth of binary branching
Provides concrete numbers demonstrating infeasibility
Names and accurately describes a viable alternative structure
Includes word-budget reasoning that supports the argument

questions.question: Compare and contrast bark systems and full dialogue trees as mechanisms for NPC communication. For each, give one scenario where it is the superior choice and explain why.
questions.answer: Bark systems deliver short, context-sensitive lines without pulling the player out of gameplay; they are superior for ambient world-building during exploration or combat because they maintain flow and do not break pacing (e.g., guards commenting on the weather or reacting to the player's faction reputation). Full dialogue trees are superior for pivotal story moments where the player needs to make informed choices that affect the narrative, such as negotiating an alliance or interrogating a suspect, because they allow multi-turn exchanges with branching consequences. Using a dialogue tree for ambient chatter would be intrusive, and using barks for a critical plot negotiation would strip the player of agency.

rubric.key: Accurately defines both bark systems and dialogue trees
Provides a valid scenario favoring bark systems with reasoning
Provides a valid scenario favoring dialogue trees with reasoning
Demonstrates understanding of pacing and player agency trade-offs

quizzes.topic: World-Building, Character Arcs, and Quest Design

questions.question: You are designing a single room in a post-apocalyptic game. Without using any text, dialogue, or UI prompts, describe at least four specific environmental storytelling elements you would place in the room to communicate that a family survived here for months before being forced to leave suddenly. Explain what each element communicates to the player.
questions.answer: Four elements: (1) A makeshift calendar scratched into the wall with tally marks spanning several months, communicating duration of occupation. (2) A child's drawings taped to a boarded-up window, communicating that a family with children lived here and tried to maintain normalcy. (3) Organized supply shelves that are half-emptied with some items knocked to the floor, communicating a hasty departure mid-packing. (4) A barricaded door with the barricade pushed aside from the inside, communicating that the family broke through their own defenses to flee from something that made staying more dangerous than leaving.

rubric.key: Four distinct environmental storytelling elements described
Each element clearly communicates specific narrative information
Elements collectively tell a coherent story of prolonged stay and sudden departure
No reliance on text, dialogue, or UI to convey meaning

questions.question: Explain the difference between a defined protagonist and a blank-slate avatar in terms of narrative design. Then describe one specific technique for handling a character arc when the player can make morally opposed choices at key decision points.
questions.answer: A defined protagonist has an established personality, backstory, and voice authored by the writer; the player influences their journey but does not determine their core identity (e.g., Geralt in The Witcher). A blank-slate avatar has minimal pre-authored personality, allowing the player to project their own identity and motivations onto the character (e.g., the Courier in Fallout: New Vegas). One technique for handling arcs with morally opposed choices is using arc-lock points: after accumulating enough choices in a particular moral direction, the narrative commits the character to that trajectory, closing off the opposite path. This allows early freedom while still delivering a coherent arc in the final act, because the character's accumulated choices crystallize into a defined identity.

rubric.key: Accurate definition and example of defined protagonist
Accurate definition and example of blank-slate avatar
Describes a specific, viable technique for handling morally opposed choices
Explains how the technique preserves arc coherence

major_assignments.title: Branching Narrative Prototype in Twine
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a branching interactive narrative of at least 3,000 words using Twine. The story must feature a minimum of three meaningful branching points that lead to at least four distinct endings. The narrative must demonstrate deliberate structural choices (foldback, parallel paths, or quality-based) rather than naive binary branching.
objectives: Apply branching narrative theory to a concrete implementation
Manage narrative scope through deliberate structural design
Write dialogue and prose that adapts to player choice while maintaining voice consistency
Demonstrate proficiency with Twine as a narrative prototyping tool
deliverables: Exported Twine HTML file playable in any browser
Narrative structure diagram showing all nodes and edges with annotations
Design document (2-3 pages) explaining structural choices and their rationale
Word count breakdown by path showing content distribution

criteria: Compelling premise and conflict
Consistent character voice across branches
Choices feel meaningful and consequential
Endings are distinct and earned by player decisions
Deliberate use of a named branching structure
Efficient use of word budget across paths
State tracking used to personalize content
No dead ends or orphaned nodes
Twine project compiles and runs without errors
Variables and conditions used correctly
Passage linking is correct throughout
Proper use of Twine macros and formatting
Structure diagram is accurate and readable
Design rationale is well-argued
Structural trade-offs are acknowledged
Professional writing and formatting
Comprehensive branching narrative with meaningful player agency
Deliberate structural design that manages scope effectively
High-quality writing with consistent voice across branches
Professional documentation and structural diagrams

midterm_exam.format: Mixed format: analytical essay questions and a timed narrative design exercise

questions.question: Analyze the narrative structure of a game you have played this semester. Identify its branching architecture, explain how it manages combinatorial complexity, and evaluate how effectively player choices translate into meaningful narrative consequences.
questions.expected_answer: A strong answer will name a specific game, correctly identify its narrative structure (foldback, hub-and-spoke, quality-based, etc.), explain the specific techniques it uses to limit branch proliferation (reconvergence points, state variables instead of unique branches, modular story segments), and critically evaluate whether the choices feel meaningful or illusory, citing specific examples from gameplay.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the branching architecture with proper terminology
Explains complexity management techniques with specific examples
Evaluates player agency and narrative consequence quality
Writing clarity and analytical depth

questions.question: Compare environmental storytelling and dialogue-driven storytelling as narrative delivery mechanisms. Discuss the strengths and limitations of each, and describe a scenario where combining both would be more effective than either alone.
questions.expected_answer: Environmental storytelling excels at world-building, atmosphere, and optional lore discovery without interrupting gameplay, but struggles to convey complex character motivations or time-sensitive plot information. Dialogue-driven storytelling excels at character development, exposition, and presenting player choices, but can break pacing and feel intrusive. A combined scenario: a detective game where the player examines a crime scene (environmental storytelling reveals physical evidence and timeline) and then interrogates a suspect (dialogue tree where the player can reference discovered evidence to unlock new dialogue branches).

rubric.key: Accurately describes strengths and limitations of environmental storytelling
Accurately describes strengths and limitations of dialogue-driven storytelling
Presents a compelling combined scenario with clear synergy
Writing clarity and depth of analysis

exercises.title: Timed Narrative Design Exercise
exercises.description: Given a one-paragraph game premise, design a complete quest chain with branching narrative. Produce a node diagram, write the opening dialogue scene in Ink syntax, and annotate your structural choices.
requirements: Node diagram with at least 8 nodes and 3 branch points
Opening scene written in valid Ink syntax with at least 2 conditional branches

project.title: Interactive Narrative Experience
project.overview: Students will design and implement a polished interactive narrative experience using the narrative tool of their choice (Twine, Ink, Yarn Spinner, or a custom engine integration). The project must demonstrate mastery of branching structure, dialogue design, environmental storytelling concepts, and character arc management.

minimum_requirements: Interactive narrative of at least 8,000 words across all paths
At least 5 meaningful branching points with state tracking
At least 6 distinct endings that reflect accumulated player choices
Environmental storytelling documentation for at least 3 locations
requirements: Original narrative concept with compelling premise
Deliberate branching architecture with documented rationale
Consistent character voices across all paths
State tracking system that personalizes content based on prior choices

criteria: Compelling story with thematic depth
Consistent and distinctive character voices
Meaningful choices with visible consequences
Multiple endings that feel earned and distinct
Writing quality at a professional level
Appropriate branching architecture for the narrative
Effective management of combinatorial complexity
State tracking used to personalize the experience
No dead ends, broken links, or orphaned content
Efficient use of content across paths
Project runs without errors on target platform
Correct use of chosen narrative tool features
Variables and conditions function correctly
Clean, organized project structure
Original concept that pushes beyond conventional genres
Innovative use of interactivity as a storytelling device
Environmental storytelling concepts applied effectively
Emotional resonance and player engagement
Complete and accurate narrative design document
Clear structural diagrams
Effective live presentation
Thoughtful post-mortem analysis

submission_requirements: Playable build or exported HTML file
Narrative design document with structure diagrams
bonus_opportunities: Integration with a game engine (Unity/Unreal) for visual presentation
Voice acting or audio design for key scenes
Procedural narrative elements that generate unique content
Localization of the narrative into a second language

required_textbooks.title: The Game Narrative Toolbox
required_textbooks.usage: Primary reference for interactive narrative design theory and practice

required_textbooks.title: Interactive Storytelling for Video Games
required_textbooks.usage: Supplementary reference for branching structures and player agency

supplementary_resources: Emily Short's blog on interactive fiction design
GDC Narrative Summit talks (available via GDC Vault)

participation_and_workshops.description: Active participation in writing workshops, peer feedback sessions, and in-class exercises

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of narrative design theory and tool proficiency

major_assignments.description: Branching narrative prototype demonstrating structural design and writing quality

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of narrative theory, structural analysis, and timed design exercise

final_project.description: Capstone interactive narrative experience demonstrating mastery of all course concepts

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all workshop and peer review sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction of one full letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late narrative assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior written arrangement with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Writing must be original and individual unless a group project is explicitly assigned. Peer feedback during workshops is expected and graded, but incorporating another student's prose without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All narrative content must be your own original work. Use of generative AI tools for drafting narrative text must be disclosed and is limited to brainstorming; submitted prose must be human-authored. Plagiarism or undisclosed AI-generated text will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a laptop capable of running Twine 2 and Inky. Yarn Spinner requires Unity (free Personal edition). All tools are free and cross-platform.
